question: According to wikipedia, how many Asian countries still have a monarchy and access to the sea in 2021?

Here is the final answer from your managed agent 'search_agent':
### 1. Task outcome (short version):
As of 2021, the following Asian countries have a monarchy:
- **Thailand**: Has access to the sea.
- **Malaysia**: Has access to the sea.
- **Brunei Darussalam**: Has access to the sea.
- **Cambodia**: Has access to the sea.
- **Bhutan**: Landlocked and does not have access to the sea.
- **Japan**: Has access to the sea.
- **Saudi Arabia**: Has access to the sea.
- **Kuwait**: Has access to the sea.

### 2. Task outcome (extremely detailed version):
Monarchies present in Asia as of 2021 include several countries where kings or sultans play significant cultural and ceremonial roles:
1. **Thailand** - A constitutional monarchy where the monarch is King Maha Vajiralongkorn. Thailand is located in Southeast Asia and has a coastline along the Andaman Sea and the Gulf of Thailand, thus having access to the sea.
2. **Malaysia** - Practices a rotating monarchy with the current Yang di-Pertuan Agong (King) from the Malay states. Malaysia also has access to the sea through both the South China Sea and the Straits of Malacca. 
3. **Brunei Darussalam** - An absolute monarchy ruled by Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah. Brunei is located on the island of Borneo and has a coastline along the South China Sea, enjoying direct access to the sea.
4. **Cambodia** - The monarchy is headed by King Norodom Sihamoni, serving mainly ceremonial duties. Cambodia has access to the sea via the Gulf of Thailand.
5. **Bhutan** - A constitutional monarchy with a king presiding over a landlocked nation, Bhutan is situated in the eastern Himalayas and does not have access to any sea.
6. **Japan** - A constitutional monarchy ruled by Emperor Naruhito, Japan is an island nation surrounded by the Pacific Ocean, hence it has significant access to the sea.
7. **Saudi Arabia** - As an absolute monarchy, it is ruled by King Salman bin Abdulaziz Al Saud. Saudi Arabia has extensive coastlines along both the Red Sea and the Arabian Gulf.
8. **Kuwait** - A constitutional emirate with Emir Nawaf Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah as its ruler, Kuwait also has its coastline along the Arabian Gulf, thus having significant access to the sea.

### 3. Additional context (if relevant):
The role of monarchies in these nations varies from ceremonial and cultural representations to actual governing power; with Brunei standing out as the only remaining absolute monarchy in the region. Monarchies have roots tied in national identity and contribute to cultural continuity and social structure in these countries.

[{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Observation:\nAddress: https://www.seasia.co/2025/05/18/these-southeast-asian-countries-still-have-kings-today\nViewport position: Showing page 7 of 18.\n=======================\nThe search string \'countries with monarchies\' was not found on this page.\nAddress: https://www.seasia.co/2025/05/18/these-southeast-asian-countries-still-have-kings-today\nViewport position: Showing page 7 of 18.\n=======================\ndata-id="32161" data-type="content" target="_blank" href="https://www.linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite/?url=https://seasia.co/2025/05/18/these-southeast-asian-countries-still-have-kings-today">\n                                        <i class="elegant-icon social_linkedin "></i>\n                                    </a>\n                                </li>\n                            </ul>\n                        </div>\n                    </div>\n                </div>\n                <!--end single header-->\n                <figure class="image mb-30 m-auto text-center border-radius-10">\n                    <img class="border-radius-10 lazy" data-src="https://seasia.co/img/articles/2025/05/18/these-southeast-asian-countries-still-have-kings-today-j2v6MnyJAO.jpg?p=articles-md" src="https://seasia.co/front/img/placeholder-857x400.jpg?v=202315-0723-0054" alt="These Southeast Asian Countries Still Have Kings Today"/>\n                    <figcaption class="mt-20">\n                        Photo by Bradley Prentice on Unsplash  Go to Bradley Prentice's profile Bradley\n                    </figcaption>\n                </figure>\n                <!--figure-->\n\n                <div class="mt-5 text-center" id="que-target"></div>\n\n                <article class="entry-wraper mb-50">\n                    <div class="entry-main-content ">\n\n                        <p>When you think of Southeast Asia, your mind might jump to vibrant street food, bustling cities, or tropical getaways. But tucked between republics and democracies are nations where royalty still reigns. While monarchies around the world have mostly become symbols or faded entirely, several countries in Southeast Asia maintain royal institutions that are very much alive, both culturally and constitutionally.</p>  <p>In fact, four ASEAN countries still have reigning monarchs: <strong>Thailand, Malaysia, Brunei Darussalam, and Cambodia</strong>. Each kingdom reflects a unique blend of history, religion, and modern statecraft that continues to shape national identity in the 21st century.</p>  <h2>Thailand: The World\'s Most Prominent Constitutional Monarchy</h2>  <p>Thailand is perhaps the most internationally recognized monarchy in Southeast Asia. Its royal institution is deeply rooted in centuries-old tradition, intertwined with Theravada Buddhism and Thai national identity. Although Thailand is a constitutional monarchy, the king holds immense cultural reverence and influence.</p><div class=\'text-center\'><div id="que-target-2"></div></div>  <p>The current monarch, <strong>King Maha Vajiralongkorn</strong> (Rama X), ascended the throne in 2016 following the death of his father, King Bhumibol Adulyadej, who was the longest-reigning monarch in Thai history. The monarchy is protected under <strong>strict lèse-majesté laws</strong>, making public criticism of the king or royal family punishable by law.</p>  <p>The Thai monarchy\'s role may be symbolic on paper, but it continues to play an active role in national unity, ceremonial traditions, and even political discourse. Portraits of the king can be found in homes, offices, and public spaces throughout the country, reflecting a strong emotional and symbolic connection between the monarchy and its people.</p><div class=\'text-center\'><div id="que-target-3"></div></div>  <h2>Malaysia: A Rotating Kingship Like No Other</h2>  <p>Malaysia offers one of the most unique systems of monarchy in the world. Instead of a single hereditary line, the country practices a <strong>rotational monarchy</strong> among the nine hereditary rulers of the Malay states. Every five years, a new <strong>Yang di-Pertuan Agong</strong> (King) is elected from among them to serve as the constitutional monarch.</p>  <p>This system has been in place since Malaysia’s independence in 1957 and represents a fusion of tradition and modern federal governance. The current Yang di-Pertuan Agong, <strong>Sultan Abdullah of Pahang</strong>, began his term in 2019.</p>  <p>While the king’s powers are largely ceremonial, he serves as the <strong>head of state</strong> and plays key roles in appointing the prime minister and other national positions during times of political uncertainty. The monarchy in Malaysia is seen as a unifying force among the diverse ethnic and religious groups in the country.</p>  <h2>Brunei Darussalam: The Last Absolute Monarchy in Southeast Asia</h2>  <p>Brunei stands out as the only <strong>absolute monarchy</strong> in Southeast Asia today. <strong>Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah</strong> has ruled the tiny but wealthy nation since 1967, making him one of the longest-reigning and wealthiest monarchs in the world.</p>  <p>Unlike Thailand or Malaysia, Brunei\'s sultan holds both <strong>executive and legislative powers</strong>. He is the head of state, the head of government, the minister of defense, and the minister of finance. In 2014, Brunei drew international attention by implementing parts of <strong>Sharia law</strong> into its legal system.</p>  <p>Despite criticism from international observers, many Bruneians express support for the monarchy, citing'}]

class="post-thumb position-relative border-radius-5">\n                        <div class="img-hover-slide border-radius-5 position-relative lazy" data-bg="https://seasia.co/img/articles/2025/07/07/southeast-asias-living-monarchies-royals-in-the-modern-age-j2If1LE1iM.jpg?p=articles-sm" style="background-image: url(https://seasia.co/front/img/placeholder-450x150.jpg?v=202315-0723-0054">\n                            <a class="img-link" href="https://seasia.co/2025/07/07/southeast-asias-living-monarchies-royals-in-the-modern-age" title="Southeast Asia’s Living Monarchies: Royals in the Modern Age"></a>\n                        </div>\n                        <ul class="social-share">\n                            <li><a href="#"><i class="elegant-icon\nAddress: https://www.seasia.co/2025/05/18/these-southeast-asian-countries-still-have-kings-today\nViewport position: Showing page 8 of 18.\n=======================\nthe country’s high standard of living, low crime rates, and generous welfare policies as indicators of the sultan’s leadership. The monarchy is seen not just as a symbol, but as the literal heart of Brunei’s government and society.</p>  <h2>Cambodia: A Kingdom Restored</h2>  <p>Cambodia’s monarchy has had a tumultuous history. Once abolished during the Khmer Rouge regime in the 1970s, the monarchy was <strong>restored in 1993</strong> as part of the country’s transition back to a constitutional system with democratic elections.</p>  <p>The current monarch, <strong>King Norodom Sihamoni</strong>, is the son of the late King Norodom Sihanouk and has reigned since 2004. King Sihamoni is known for his calm and apolitical presence, often staying out of direct government matters while serving as a symbol of national unity and continuity.</p>  <p>Although the king has no executive power, he is seen as a <strong>spiritual and moral leader</strong>, performing ceremonial duties and promoting national identity, culture, and the arts. The Cambodian monarchy today is symbolic, but its legacy remains central to the nation’s sense of history and healing.</p>  <h2>More Than Just Symbols</h2>  <p>While none of these monarchs hold unchecked power (except in Brunei), their presence continues to influence public life, politics, and cultural identity. In times of crisis or political transition, monarchs in countries like Malaysia and Thailand often play stabilizing roles.</p>  <p>Beyond governance, monarchies also serve as <strong>cultural anchors</strong>. They embody national pride, religious values, and traditions that stretch back centuries. Whether through royal ceremonies, public holidays, or everyday symbols like currency and postage stamps, the monarchy remains visible and relevant in everyday life.</p>  <h2>Monarchies in a Modern World</h2>  <p>In a world where monarchies are increasingly rare, Southeast Asia presents a fascinating contrast. Here, royal families are not just remnants of the past. They are living institutions that continue to evolve and adapt.</p>  <p>As political systems grow more complex and populations more diverse, the role of monarchies in these nations serves as a reminder that tradition and modernity can coexist. Kings may not govern in the traditional sense, but in many ways, they still reign in the hearts of their people.</p>\n                    </div>\n                    <div class="mt-20 mb-30">\n
